reasoning: The question asks for the probability of the October 2025 global temperature anomaly falling into a specific, narrow range (1.10ºC to 1.14ºC) according to the NASA GISS dataset. Because this is a 0.05ºC-wide bucket, no single bucket will hold a very high probability given the inherent volatility of monthly temperature anomalies. October 2025 is forecast to occur during La Niña conditions (71% probability), which typically exhibit cooling effects globally compared to El Niño years. Considering the 2023 October anomaly reached approximately +1.34ºC during El Niño conditions, a shift to a La Niña regime in late 2025 combined with baseline warming trends makes an anomaly in the 1.00ºC - 1.20ºC range very plausible. A normal distribution of expected temperatures centered around 1.10-1.15ºC with a typical monthly standard deviation of ~0.08ºC suggests that the 1.10-1.14ºC bucket would capture approximately 10-18% of the probability mass. Without precise readings from August/September 2025 to definitively narrow the variance, the probability should align with a slightly broad distribution over multiple neighboring temperature brackets. I estimate a 13% chance that the final reported value falls specifically within this 5-hundredths of a degree window.
